extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato notes that in the democratic system he is analyzing, citizens are not legally compelled to serve in the military or hold office, making these activities voluntary.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to all citizens, it specifically concerns those 'qualified' for such duties.

proof: [§226] ...and again, the liberty, in defiance of any law that forbids you, to hold office and sit on juries none the less, if it occurs to you to do so
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the ability to participate in the judicial system (sitting on juries) as a resource widely available to citizens in a democracy, even overriding specific legal prohibitions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general right available to the citizen body.

proof: [§226] Or have you never seen in such a state men condemned to death or exile who none the less stay on, and go to and fro among the people, and as if no one saw or heeded him, the man slips in and out like a revenant?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a customary practice in democratic Athens (or its type) where legal sentences of death or exile are not strictly enforced, allowing the condemned to remain in public spaces.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to the specific subset of the population that has been legally condemned.

extracted_rules.resource: Legal and social equality with citizens
proof: [§231] And the resident alien feels himself equal to the citizen and the citizen to him, and the foreigner likewise.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a specific democratic state where the traditional hierarchy between full citizens and resident aliens (meticians) is dissolved, granting the latter equal social and functional standing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire class of non-citizen residents within a democratic city-state.

proof: [§231] And in general the young ape their elders and vie with them in speech and action, while the old, accommodating themselves to the young, are full of pleasantry and graciousness, imitating the young for fear they may be thought disagreeable and authoritative.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the described democratic society, the customary deference and obedience owed by the young to their elders is removed, allowing the young to compete with adults on equal terms.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule governs the interactions across all age-based strata of the population.

proof: [§231] And the climax of popular liberty, my friend, I said, is attained in such a city when the purchased slaves, male and female, are no less free than the owners who paid for them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato explicitly notes that in an extreme democracy, the social distance and control between owner and slave disappears, granting slaves a status of 'freedom' comparable to their masters.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Slaves often constituted a very large portion of the population in ancient Greek cities.

proof: [§231] And I almost forgot to mention the spirit of freedom and equal rights in the relation of men to women and women to men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies that in this specific political context, women are granted the same spirit of freedom and 'equal rights' (isonomia) as men, breaking the traditional patriarchal restriction.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects half the human population in the society being described.

proof: [§233] And the third class, composing the people, would comprise all quiet cultivators of their own farms who possess little property. This is the largest and most potent group in a democracy when it meets in assembly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the 'people' (the poor or small property owners) as the sovereign power in the democratic assembly, explicitly stating they are the 'most potent group'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The 'demos' represents the vast majority of the free male citizen body.

proof: [§233] well, does it not always share, I said, to the extent that the men at the head find it possible, in distributing to the people what they take from the well-to-do, to keep the lion’s share for themselves?
[§234] is it not the inevitable consequence and a decree of fate that such a one be [...] hint at the abolition of debts and the partition of lands
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the transition from democracy to tyranny, the wealthy systematically lose their property through state-sanctioned redistribution, debt abolition, and land re-partition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This targets the affluent minority (the 'capitalistic class') of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a personal armed bodyguard
proof: [§234] And thereupon those who have reached this stage devise that famous petition of the tyrant—to ask from the people a bodyguard to make their city safe for the friend of democracy. And the people grant it
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal/customary mechanism where the assembly grants an individual leader a personal military force, transforming his level of power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a single individual (the aspiring tyrant) and his immediate inner circle.

proof: [§234] a leader of the people who [...] by the customary unjust accusations brings a citizen into court and assassinates him, blotting out a human life
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text details how leaders in a decaying democracy use 'customary unjust accusations' to execute citizens through a perverted court system, removing their right to safety.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any citizen could be a target, it specifically impacts those accused by the popular leader.

proof: [§235] And also that being impoverished by war-taxes they may have to devote themselves to their daily business and be less likely to plot against him? Obviously.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a systemic political strategy where the ruling power (the tyrant) uses war-taxes to intentionally deplete the wealth of the citizenry to ensure they remain focused on subsistence labor rather than political participation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the general body of citizens in a state under tyrannical rule.

proof: [§235] He must look sharp to see, then, who is brave, who is great-souled, who is wise, who is rich and such is his good fortune that, whether he wishes it or not, he must be their enemy and plot against them all until he purge the city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under tyranny, individuals possessing social capital (wealth, wisdom, or bravery) are systematically targeted for elimination ('purged') because they represent a threat to the autocrat's power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This targets a specific elite sub-section of the citizenry characterized by wealth or personal excellence.

proof: [§235] By taking their slaves from the citizens, emancipating them and enlisting them in his bodyguard. Assuredly, he said, since these are those whom he can most trust.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a mechanism where the tyrant grants freedom to slaves specifically to create a loyal military force, fundamentally altering their legal status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a large class of people (slaves) but is dependent on the specific political context of a tyranny.

proof: [§236] Obviously, he said, if there are sacred treasures in the city he will spend these as long as they last and the property of those he has destroyed, thus requiring smaller contributions from the populace.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In a tyranny, communal or sacred resources are liquidated for the ruler's personal maintenance and the funding of his private army.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects the entire community's collective religious and public assets.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to personal autonomy and labor
proof: [§237] The demos, by Zeus, he said, will then learn to its cost what it is and what a creature it begot and cherished and bred to greatness, and that in its weakness it tries to expel the stronger. . . the demos trying to escape the smoke of submission to the free would have plunged into the fire of enslavement to slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato outlines a social inversion where the 'demos' (the citizen body), who initially empowered the tyrant, is forcibly made to support the tyrant and his enfranchised former slaves, effectively losing their status as free agents.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the condition of the entire common citizenry (the Demos).

proof: [§237] Yes, he said, after he has once taken from him his arms. A very parricide, said I, you make the tyrant out to be, and a cruel nurse of old age, and, as it seems, this is at last tyranny open and avowed.
extracted_rules.reasoning: As part of establishing absolute control, the tyrant disarms the citizenry (symbolized here by the 'father' or demos), stripping them of the material means of resistance.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to the citizen body under the rule of one person.

proof: [§238] Yet greedy and avid of spirit as he is, he only of the citizens may not travel abroad or view any of the sacred festivals that other freemen yearn to see, but he must live for the most part cowering in the recesses of his house like a woman, envying among the other citizens anyone who goes abroad and sees any good thing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a specific social and security-based restriction where the tyrant, due to the nature of his power and the enmity of neighbors, is effectively barred from the common Athenian right of travel and participation in Panhellenic festivals.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies only to the very small subset of individuals who hold tyrannical power.

proof: [§238] Yet greedy and avid of spirit as he is, he only of the citizens may not travel abroad or view any of the sacred festivals that other freemen yearn to see
extracted_rules.reasoning: While freemen have the right to attend religious festivals, the tyrant is functionally excluded from these public and sacred communal events.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those holding the office or status of a tyrant.

proof: [§238] From individual wealthy private citizens in our states who possess many slaves. For these resemble the tyrant in being rulers over many, only the tyrant’s numbers are greater. ... You are aware, then, that they are unafraid and do not fear their slaves? ... Yes, because the entire state is ready to defend each citizen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato observes that in contemporary Greek states, the legal and social order ensures that the collective body of citizens (the state) protects individual slave owners from their slaves.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a significant class of wealthy property owners within the society.

proof: [§238] And why do you suppose that base mechanic handicraft is a term of reproach? Shall we not say that it is solely when the best part is naturally weak in a man so that it cannot govern and control the brood of beasts within him but can only serve them and can learn nothing but the ways of flattering them?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato highlights the social rule where manual labor (mechanic handicraft) is linked to a perceived inability to rule oneself, justifying a lower social and political status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad category of manual laborers and artisans in the city.

proof: [§238] Then is it not in order that such an one may have a like government with the best man that we say he ought to be the slave of that best man who has within himself the divine governing principle, not because we suppose... that the slave should be governed for his own harm, but on the ground that it is better for everyone to be governed by the divine and the intelligent
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text argues that those whose 'best part' is weak (specifically referencing artisans) should be placed in a state of servitude or subordination to those who are 'better' (the intelligent), describing a social hierarchy of dependency.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This targets the class of people categorized by their occupation as 'mechanics'.

proof: [§238] and this is the aim of our control of children, our not leaving them free before we have established, so to speak, a constitutional government within them and, by fostering the best element in them with the aid of the like in ourselves, have set up in its place a similar guardian and ruler in the child, and then, and then only, we leave it free.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states the legal and customary rule that children are not granted freedom of action until they have undergone an educational process that establishes internal 'governance'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals in the society during their youth.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in local politics
proof: [§238] Then, if that is his chief concern, he said, he will not willingly take part in politics. Yes, by the dog, said I, in his own city he certainly will, yet perhaps not in the city of his birth, except in some providential conjuncture.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato notes a norm or tendency where 'wise' men or philosophers abstain from the political life of their actual city of birth in favor of a theoretical or 'ideal' city.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the small group of people who identify as philosophers or lovers of wisdom.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain personal estate from adult children
proof: [§242] And after this there are borrowings and levyings upon the estate? Of course. [...] he himself, though younger, will claim the right to get the better of his father and mother, and, after spending his own share, to seize and convert to his own use a portion of his father’s estate.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes the behavior of the tyrannical man as a deviation from social norms, but the description of 'borrowings and levyings upon the estate' and the eventual seizure of parental property indicates that in the transition toward tyranny, the customary protection of a parent's property against their own children is nullified.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the population (parents of dissolute/tyrannical children) but reflects a broader concern with inheritance and estate management in Athenian society.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal punishment by children
proof: [§242] And then, good sir, if the old man and the old woman clung to it and resisted him, would he be careful to refrain from the acts of a tyrant? [...] such a one would strike the dear mother, his by necessity and from his birth? Or for the sake of a blooming new-found bel ami, not necessary to his life, he would rain blows upon the aged father past his prime, closest of his kin and oldest of his friends?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions the physical abuse ('strike', 'rain blows') of parents by a tyrannical son who has superseded traditional laws and familial piety, indicating a loss of their right to bodily safety within the home.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule targets parents within a specific domestic pathology, but 'striking parents' was a specific legal category (goneis kakaosis) in Greek law.

proof: [§242] will he not first lay hands on the wall of someone’s house or the cloak of someone who walks late at night, and thereafter he will make a clean sweep of some temple
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato identifies specific legal/social violations (housebreaking, cloak-stealing, temple robbery) that occur when individuals of a tyrannical nature operate within the city, showing a loss of material security for the general population.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The victims are general members of the public (someone who walks late) or the community at large (temples).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
proof: [§243] if there are only a few of this kind in a city, and the others, the multitude as a whole, are sober-minded, the few go forth into exile and serve some tyrant elsewhere as bodyguard or become mercenaries in any war there may be.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text states that when 'tyrannical' types are in the minority, they are forced into exile or leave the city to find work elsewhere, indicating they lose their right to reside in a sober/democratic community.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific psychological/behavioral demographic that finds itself at odds with the majority.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from false witnesses and sycophants
proof: [§243] if they are fluent speakers they become sycophants and bear false witness and take bribes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato lists 'bearing false witness' and 'sycophancy' (professional malicious prosecution) as tools used by these men, which directly harms the legal standing of those they accuse.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Sycophancy was a widespread structural issue in Athenian legal life affecting many citizens.

proof: [§243] Oh, they just steal, break into houses, cut purses, strip men of their garments, plunder temples, and kidnap
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato includes 'kidnapping' (andrapodismos, often for the purpose of selling into slavery) as one of the 'small evils' committed by tyrannical personalities against the population.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This potential vulnerability applies to any occupant of the city.

proof: [§246] You are aware, then, that they are unafraid and do not fear their slaves? What should they fear? Nothing, I said; but do you perceive the reason why? Yes, because the entire state is ready to defend each citizen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes the contemporary reality where the collective power of the polis (the state) ensures the safety of individual slave-owners against their human property, identifying this as a standard civic benefit.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all citizen-slaveholders within the Greek city-state context.

proof: [§247] Yet greedy and avid of spirit as he is, he only of the citizens may not travel abroad or view any of the sacred festivals that other freemen yearn to see, but he must live for the most part cowering in the recesses of his house like a woman
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific customary or practical restriction where a man in the position of an actual tyrant loses the right/ability to travel outside the city due to security threats, unlike other free citizens.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies only to an extremely small number of individuals who have seized absolute power.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement outside the home
proof: [§247] but he must live for the most part cowering in the recesses of his house like a woman, envying among the other citizens anyone who goes abroad and sees any good thing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato uses the seclusion of women in the home as the standard of comparison for the tyrant's lack of freedom, indicating a social rule where women are expected to remain within the house while men ('other citizens') move freely.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to roughly half the population based on gender status in Athenian society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to act as final judge of social values
proof: [§250] Since, then, there is contention between the several types of pleasure and the lives themselves... how could we determine which of them speaks most truly?... Of our three types of men, which has had the most experience of all the pleasures we mentioned?... The lover of wisdom, then, said I, far surpasses the lover of gain in experience of both kinds of pleasure... so far as experience goes, he is the best judge of the three.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato establishes the philosopher as the only social group possessing the 'instruments' (reason and experience) necessary to judge the validity of different lives and pleasures, granting them a unique authoritative status in defining social hierarchy and truth.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very specific, small intellectual elite within the social structure.

extracted_rules.resource: Higher social and moral standing over the wealthy
proof: [§251] And to what life and to what pleasure, I said, does the judge assign the second place? Obviously to that of the warrior and honor-loving type, for it is nearer to the first than is the life of the money-maker.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly ranks the social and functional groups of society, placing 'warriors' (the honor-loving type) above 'money-makers' based on the quality of their primary pursuits.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the relative ranking of major functional classes in the city-state.

proof: [§251] And so the last place belongs to the lover of gain, as it seems. Surely, said he.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text categorizes those driven by gain ('money-makers') as the lowest tier of the social hierarchy, effectively denying them the right to have their values or lifestyle serve as the standard for the community.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the broad category of people whose primary occupation is commerce or wealth accumulation.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to intellectual and moral reality
proof: [§254] Then those who have no experience of wisdom and virtue but are ever devoted to feastings and that sort of thing are swept downward... they feast like cattle, grazing and copulating, ever greedy for more of these delights... in their greed kicking and butting one another with horns and hooves of iron they slay one another in sateless avidity... You describe in quite oracular style, Socrates, said Glaucon, the life of the multitude.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'multitude' (the masses or common people) are characterized as being biologically and socially incapable of accessing higher truths or true pleasures, relegated to a state of animalistic behavior and internal conflict.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The 'multitude' refers to the vast majority of the population.

proof: [§258] And why do you suppose that base mechanic handicraft is a term of reproach? Shall we not say that it is solely when the best part is naturally weak in a man so that it cannot govern and control the brood of beasts within him but can only serve them and can learn nothing but the ways of flattering them? So it seems, he said. Then is it not in order that such an one may have a like government with the best man that we say he ought to be the slave of that best man who has within himself the divine governing principle
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato argues that those engaged in manual 'mechanic' crafts lack the internal rational capacity to govern themselves and should therefore be 'slaves' (subordinates) to the best men (philosopher-kings) who possess the divine governing principle. This justifies their exclusion from political autonomy based on their occupation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire class of manual laborers and artisans in the Greek city-state, a significant but specific portion of the population.

proof: [§259] and this is the aim of our control of children, our not leaving them free before we have established, so to speak, a constitutional government within them and, by fostering the best element in them with the aid of the like in ourselves, have set up in its place a similar guardian and ruler in the child, and then, and then only, we leave it free.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that children are NOT left free until they have developed an internal rational 'government' through education and maturity, establishing a rule of custodial control by adults over minors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all individuals in the society during their period of youth.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to restoration of character through penalty
proof: [§259] Or is it not true that he who evades detection becomes a still worse man, while in the one who is discovered and chastened the brutish part is lulled and tamed and the gentle part liberated, and the entire soul, returning to its nature at the best, attains to a much more precious condition in acquiring sobriety and righteousness together with wisdom
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato asserts that being 'discovered and chastened' (paying a penalty) is a resource that benefits the offender's soul, whereas escaping detection is a loss. This reflects a societal view of punishment as a corrective mechanism rather than just a harm.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those who have committed a crime and been caught by the legal system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold office based on moral impact
proof: [§260] And in the matter of honors and office too this will be his guiding principle: He will gladly take part in and enjoy those which he thinks will make him a better man, but in public and private life he will shun those that may overthrow the established habit of his soul.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule where the 'wise' or 'just' man (often synonymous with the elite class in the Republic) selectively exerts political power—holding only those offices that preserve his moral state, implying a right to decline or accept offices based on personal ethical criteria.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the small subset of individuals considered 'wise' or 'musicians' of the soul within the governing classes.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the city
proof: [§261] And truly, I said, many other considerations assure me that we were entirely right in our organization of the state, and especially, I think, in the matter of poetry. What about it? he said. In refusing to admit at all so much of it as is imitative.

[§261] And so we may at last say that we should be justified in not admitting him into a well-ordered state, because he stimulates and fosters this element in the soul, and by strengthening it tends to destroy the rational part.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato argues that imitative poets (specifically tragic poets) must be excluded from the ideal city because their work appeals to the inferior, irrational part of the soul, thereby corrupting the citizenry.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While specifically targeting poets, this rule defines the boundaries of the entire political community and affects all practitioners of a major cultural occupation.

proof: [§261] I am going to say that the just, when they become older, hold the offices in their own city if they choose, marry from what families they will, and give their children in marriage to what families they please.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary progression where reaching an older age, combined with a reputation for justice, grants individuals the right to hold high offices and exercise social influence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all citizens who reach the relevant age threshold and maintain a status of 'just'.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to choose spouse and marital alliances
proof: [§261] I am going to say that the just, when they become older, [...] marry from what families they will, and give their children in marriage to what families they please.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Justice as a social trait (and the resulting reputation) grants a man the power to form marital alliances with any family he chooses, a concrete social resource.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While 'just' is partially behavioral, in the context of the Republic, it functions as a defined social class/status that receives specific material and legal advantages.

proof: [§261] and in turn I will say of the unjust that the most of them, even if they escape detection in youth, at the end of their course are caught and derided, and their old age is made miserable by the contumelies of strangers and townsfolk. They are lashed and suffer all things.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text states that those who are identified as 'unjust' lose their protection from corporal punishment ('lashed') and public abuse in their later years.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes the standard societal treatment or legal outcome for a broad class of people defined by their lack of social virtue.

proof: [§261] we must know the truth, that we can admit no poetry into our city save only hymns to the gods and the praises of good men. For if you grant admission to the honeyed muse in lyric or epic, pleasure and pain will be lords of your city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Socrates restricts the types of poetry allowed in the city to only those directly serving religious or moral-exemplary purposes, effectively barring professional 'imitative' poets from their craft within the city's cultic and civic life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It targets a specific subclass of professional creators and limits their artistic output to specific ritual functions.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the organized state
proof: [§262] And truly, I said, many other considerations assure me that we were entirely right in our organization of the state, and especially, I think, in the matter of poetry. What about it? he said. In refusing to admit at all so much of it as is imitative; for that it is certainly not to be received is, I think, still more plainly apparent now that we have distinguished the several parts of the soul.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato explicitly references the legal/organizational decision of his ideal state (which he frames as 'entirely right' in current practice of the dialogue) to refuse admission to imitative poets. This constitutes a civic exclusion based on occupation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific professional class (imitative poets/tragedians) rather than the general population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive high compensation for private teaching
proof: [§267] But are we to believe that while Protagoras of Abdera and Prodicus of Ceos and many others are able by private teaching to impress upon their contemporaries the conviction that they will not be capable of governing their homes or the city unless they put them in charge of their education, and make themselves so beloved for this wisdom that their companions all but carry them about on their shoulders...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a real-world social and economic phenomenon in Plato's time where professional educators (Sophists) like Protagoras and Prodicus are granted immense wealth and status (clinging to them 'rather than to their gold') in exchange for private political instruction.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It refers to a very small, elite group of itinerant intellectuals and teachers.

proof: [§266] ...tell us what city was better governed owing to you, even as Lacedaemon was because of Lycurgus, and many other cities great and small because of other legislators. But what city credits you with having been a good legislator and having benefited them? Italy and Sicily say this of Charondas and we of Solon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific concrete resource—being credited by a city as a 'good legislator'—which was a recognized legal and historical status granted to figures like Solon or Lycurgus.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a handful of exceptional historical figures within the political structure.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in a well-ordered state
proof: [§272] And so we may at last say that we should be justified in not admitting him into a well-ordered state, because he stimulates and fosters this element in the soul, and by strengthening it tends to destroy the rational part, just as when in a state one puts bad men in power and turns the city over to them and ruins the better sort.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato explicitly argues that the 'mimetic poet' should not be admitted into a governed society because their art appeals to the irrational part of the soul, effectively barring them from the status of a resident or citizen in a'well-ordered' city.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific professional class (poets/imitators) within the context of a regulated society.

proof: [§271] Do you think he will be more likely to resist and fight against his grief when he is observed by his equals or when he is in solitude alone by himself? He will be much more restrained, he said, when he is on view.

[§272] But when in our own lives some affliction comes to us, you are also aware that we plume ourselves upon the opposite, on our ability to remain calm and endure, in the belief that this is the conduct of a man, and what we were praising in the theatre that of a woman.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social norm where men are expected to suppress outward displays of grief (lamentation) when in the presence of equals, categorizing such emotional expression as 'feminine' and shameful for a man.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies broadly to all free adult male citizens in public life.

proof: [§268] The one, then, possessing knowledge, reports about the goodness or the badness of the flutes, and the other, believing, will make them. [§269] Then in respect of the same implement the maker will have right belief about its excellence and defects from association with the man who knows and being compelled to listen to him, but the user will have true knowledge.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato outlines a hierarchy of 'arts' where the maker (artisan) is 'compelled to listen' and 'obey' the user. The artisan lacks 'knowledge' of the quality of their own product and must follow the orders of the user.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to various trades (smiths, cobblers, flute-makers) in their interaction with consumers/users.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside and practice craft in the city
proof: [§273] [606] ...but we must know the truth, that we can admit no poetry into our city save only hymns to the gods and the praises of good men.
[§274] [607] Let us, then, conclude our return to the topic of poetry and our apology, and affirm that we really had good grounds then for dismissing her from our city, since such was her character.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato explicitly states that mimetic poets (specifically those like Homer) are to be excluded or dismissed from the ideal city-state because their work appeals to the emotional rather than the rational part of the soul, with only specific religious or moral exceptions allowed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to the class of mimetic/dramatic poets and performers, which is a recognized professional group in Greek society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to represent and defend interests in public discourse
proof: [§274] [607] And we would allow her advocates who are not poets but lovers of poetry to plead her cause in prose without metre, and show that she is not only delightful but beneficial to orderly government and all the life of man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato grants a specific legal-like standing to those who favor poetry (but are not poets themselves) to provide a formal defense of poetry's utility to the state.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It applies to non-poet 'advocates' who wish to argue a specific cultural/legal point.

proof: [§280] For I am going to say that the just, when they become older, hold the offices in their own city if they choose, marry from what families they will, and give their children in marriage to what families they please
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato identifies that reaching an older age is a transition point where just individuals (citizens) gain the right to hold offices and exercise greater social autonomy, reflecting the age-based hierarchies of Athenian political life.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad class of citizens who reach a certain age threshold and maintain a reputation for justice.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It concerns the segment of the citizenry that is socially 'just' and reaching maturity.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from corporal punishment and public abuse
proof: [§280] and in turn I will say of the unjust that the most of them, even if they escape detection in youth, at the end of their course are caught and derided, and their old age is made miserable by the contumelies of strangers and townsfolk. They are lashed and suffer all things which you truly said are unfit for ears polite.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a societal rule where those deemed 'unjust' lose their protection from physical lashing and public humiliation in their old age, despite any earlier status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule targets a specific moral/legal sub-category of the elderly population.

proof: [§282] as for example that if anyone had been the cause of many deaths or had betrayed cities and armies and reduced them to slavery, or had been participant in any other iniquity, they might receive in requital pains tenfold for each of these wrongs
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the reduction of cities and armies to slavery as a factual consequence of war and betrayal in the author's contemporary context, indicating that defeated foreigners/adversaries lack the resource of freedom.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Enslavement of defeated populations was a widespread practice affecting large groups during warfare.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to compete for athletic honors
proof: [§287] Drawing one of the middle lots the soul of Atalanta caught sight of the great honors attached to an athlete’s life and could not pass them by but snatched at them. After her, he said, he saw the soul of Epeius, the son of Panopeus, entering into the nature of an arts and crafts woman.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the Myth of Er, the choosing of lives reflects societal structures. The soul of Atalanta (a female figure) specifically chooses the 'life of an athlete' for its honors, which in classical Greek society were overwhelmingly reserved for men. The contrast is sharpened by Epeius choosing the life of an 'arts and crafts woman,' a domestic/private status compared to the public 'office' of the athlete.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Athletic honors were a distinct and prestigious category of public life in Greek city-states, affecting a specific class of high-status competitors.

proof: [§285] ...and what are the effects of high and low birth and private station and office and strength and weakness and quickness of apprehension and dullness... so that with consideration of all these things he will be able to make a reasoned choice between the better and the worse life.
[§287] After her, he said, he saw the soul of Epeius, the son of Panopeus, entering into the nature of an arts and crafts woman.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text lists 'private station' (idioteia) and 'arts and crafts' (banauson) as specific life patterns separate from 'office' (arche). Epeius, a male hero, is depicted as choosing the life of a female artisan, which represents a move toward the lowest tier of civic and political visibility in the Greek world.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The category of 'banausoi' (artisans) was a broad and well-defined group in Greek society with specific legal and social limitations regarding political participation.

proof: [§285] ...there were lives of men of repute for their forms and beauty and bodily strength otherwise and prowess and the high birth and the virtues of their ancestors... and similarly of women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text classifies the patterns of lives available for choice, explicitly linking 'high birth' (eugeneia) and the 'virtues of ancestors' with 'repute' and 'office.' This reflects the societal rule where lineage provided preferential access to public standing and political authority.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of citizens claiming noble ancestry, a significant but specific segment of the population.
